coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention for their ability to artificially improve photosynthesis. Microalgal bioproducts have emerged as promising sources of sustainable nutrition and energy. However, the gene regulation mechanism of CDs on microalgae remains unexplored. The study synthesized red-emitting CDs and applied them to Chlamydomonas reinhardtii. Results showed that 0.5 mg/L-CDs acted as light supplements to promote cell division and biomass in C. reinhardtii. CDs improved the energy transfer of PS II, photochemical efficiency of PS II, and photosynthetic electron transfer. The pigment content and carbohydrate production slightly increased, while protein and lipid contents significantly increased (by 28.4% and 27.7%, respectively) in a short cultivation time. Transcriptome analysis identified 1166 differentially expressed genes. CDs resulted in faster cell growth by up-regulating the expression of genes associated with cell growth and death, promoting sister chromatid separation, accelerating the mitotic process and shortening the cell cycle. CDs improved the ability of energy conversion by up-regulating photosynthetic electron transfer-related genes. Carbohydrate metabolism-related genes were regulated and provided more available pyruvate for the citrate cycle. The study provides evidence for the genetic regulation of microalgal bioresources by artificially synthesized CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and properties of prepared CDs. (a) TEM and the size distribution diagram (inset), (b) HRTEM images and (c) FFT pattern of CDs; (d) XRD patterns, (e) FTIR spectra, (f) full XPS patterns, (g) UV–Vis, maximum FL excitation and emission spectra of CDs and (h) FL decay lifetime of CDs.

The promotion of growth of C. reinhardtii by CDs. (a) The dry cell weight and (b) specific growth rate of C. reinhardtii cultivated in the absence and presence of different concentrations of CDs. Optical microscopic images of (c) control and (d) 0.5 mg/L CDs treatment. Bars marked with an asterisk (*) represent treatments that show significantly different from the control at the same time point (**, P < 0.01; one-way ANOVA followed by post hoc Tukey’s multiple comparison test). Experiments were repeated three times, the error bars indicate standard deviation.

The different ultrastructure of C. reinhardtii cells in the presence and absence of CDs. Ultrastructural observation of algae for (a) control, (b) 0.5 mg/L CDs and (c) 5 mg/L CDs. C, chloroplast; G, golgi apparatus; L, liposome; N, nucleus; P, pyrenoid; S, starch; V, vacuole. Bars = 5 μm, 2 μm and 1 μm, in order from top to bottom.

The effects of the presence and absence of CDs on PSII reaction center activity and performance indexes of C. reinhardtii. (a) ABS/RC, (b) TRO/RC, (c) ETO/RC and (d) DIO/RC in control and CDs (0.5 and 5 mg/L) treatments. (e) A highly simplified scheme of energy cascade from light absorption to electron transport. (f) PIabs of C. reinhardtii in control and different CDs concentrations groups. Bars marked with an asterisk (*) represent treatments that show significantly different from the control at the same time point (*, P < 0.05; one-way ANOVA followed by post hoc Tukey’s multiple comparison test). Experiments were repeated three times, the error bars indicate standard deviation.

Response of basic fluorescence parameters and electron transfer to different CDs treatments. The spider plot of chlorophyll fluorescence parameters of C. reinhardtii and C. reinhardtii /CDs on (a) Day 2, (b) Day 4 and (c) Day 6, respectively. (d) Fv/Fm (e) ΦPSII and (f) rETR of C. reinhardtii and C. reinhardtii /CDs. Bars marked with an asterisk (*) represent treatments that show significantly different from the control at the same time point (*, P < 0.05; one-way ANOVA followed by post hoc Tukey’s multiple comparison test). Experiments were repeated three times, the error bars indicate standard deviation.

The effect of CDs on photosynthetic pigments of C. reinhardtii. The cellular content of (a) chlorophyll a, (b) chlorophyll b, (c) carotenoid and (d) pigment of control and different CDs treatments, respectively. Bars marked with an asterisk (*) represent treatments that show significantly different from the control at the same time point (*, P < 0.05; one-way ANOVA followed by post hoc Tukey’s multiple comparison test). Experiments were repeated three times, the error bars indicate standard deviation.

The improvement of CDs on the main metabolites of C. reinhardtii. The (a and b) protein, (c and d) carbohydrate, and (e and f) lipid contents (line chart), production (column chart) and productivity in C. reinhardtii cultured in the presence and absence of CDs. Bars marked with an asterisk (*) represent treatments that show significantly different from the control at the same time point (*, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01; ***, P < 0.001; one-way ANOVA followed by post hoc Tukey’s multiple comparison test). Experiments were repeated three times, the error bars indicate standard deviation.

RNA-Seq analysis of C. reinhardtii treated by 0.5 mg/L CDs. (a) Volcano plot of DEGs analysis. (b) KEGG pathway analysis for DEGs analysis. The top 10 enriched pathways of (c) up-regulated genes and (d) down-regulated genes of C. reinhardtii by KEGG.

Regulation of gene expression in pathways associated with cell growth and death, energy metabolism and carbohydrate metabolism in C. reinhardtii under 0.5 mg/L CDs treatment. (a) Heat map of enrichment genes of the KEGG. (b) The sanky plot of gene information of the KEGG. Con-1,2,3 are three parallel samples of the control group. CDs-1,2,3 are three parallel samples of 0.5 mg/L CDs-treatment group.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention for their ability to artificially improve photosynthesis. Microalgal bioproducts have emerged as promising sources of sustainable nutrition and energy. However, the gene regulation mechanism of CDs on microalgae remains unexplored. The study synthesized red-emitting CDs and applied them to Chlamydomonas reinhardtii. Results showed that 0.5 mg/L-CDs acted as light supplements to promote cell division and biomass in C. reinhardtii. CDs improved the energy transfer of PS II, photochemical efficiency of PS II, and photosynthetic electron transfer. The pigment content and carbohydrate production slightly increased, while protein and lipid contents significantly increased (by 28.4% and 27.7%, respectively) in a short cultivation time. Transcriptome analysis identified 1166 differentially expressed genes. CDs resulted in faster cell growth by up-regulating the expression of genes associated with cell growth and death, promoting sister chromatid separation, accelerating the mitotic process and shortening the cell cycle. CDs improved the ability of energy conversion by up-regulating photosynthetic electron transfer-related genes. Carbohydrate metabolism-related genes were regulated and provided more available pyruvate for the citrate cycle. The study provides evidence for the genetic regulation of microalgal bioresources by artificially synthesized CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Agriculture is one of the oldest economic sectors of mankind. Nevertheless, there are still many countries suffering from undernutrition today [1]. Scientists predict that the population will reach 9 billion by 2050 [2], placing an increasingly heavy burden on arable land [3]. Furthermore, disease outbreaks—such as the novel coronavirus pneumonia epidemic that emerged in 2020—are increasingly leading to the instability of food supply chains [4]. These pressures require us to find ways to reliably meet the ever increasing demand for high-nutritional value products amidst declining natural resources.

Microalgae, which can accumulate pigments, proteins and other by-products through photosynthesis [5], have emerged as one potential sustainable resource for high-value bioproducts. The advantages of microalgae cultivation are tremendous (no need for arable land; can be grown in freshwater, brackish water, and even wastewater environments; display high growth rates [6]). Potential applications of microalgae products are wide-ranging, including as functional food [5], in environmental remediation [7], and for the prevention and treatment of diseases [8]. Chlamydomonas reinhardtii is considered a model study organism as it was among the first of the engineered algal species to be studied in commercial settings [9,10], has a short generational time, and its nuclear and chloroplast genomes have been sequenced and annotated.

Photosynthetic organisms absorb light and transfer excited-state energy to reaction centers where the photochemical and electron-transfer reactions of photosynthesis occur [11,12]. The reaction comprises the fixing of inorganic carbon through carbon capture machinery (CCM) that regulates the assimilation and accumulation of carbon [13]. Pigment molecules, mainly chlorophyll, absorb the 400 ∼ 500 nm and 650 ∼ 800 nm wavebands of the electromagnetic spectrum, which provide the power for photosynthesis [14]. However, less than 10% of sunlight is used for photosynthesis [15], and only 1% of the total energy absorbed is used for energy conversion [16]. The remaining energy is lost in the vast reactions of photosynthetic organisms to maintain metabolism and survive and reproduce [16,17]. Therefore, research on how to improve the efficiency of photosynthesis in microalgae is considered essential for energy exploitation and bio-resource applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials that have garnered interest because of their unique properties (e.g., extremely small size, easy synthesis, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and biodegradability) and as potential electron acceptors and donors [18–20]. Their optical absorption and emission properties (which range from the UV to the IR spectrum) can be precisely tuned, conferring large stokes shifts, high stability, and photonic conversion efficiency by varying the composition and the nanocrystal size [21,22]. Currently, many strategies (gene regulation [23], genetic modification [24], and environmental modeling [25]) have been developed to improve photosynthetic efficiency, but most are costly and not universally applicable [26]. Conversely, CDs appear to have prominent advantages as photoluminescent antennae or as mediums for energy transfer to enhance plant growth and resilience and improve photosynthetic efficiency [27,28]. However, minimal research exists on the role of CDs in expanding the production of microalgal biomass resources, and the mechanism by which CDs increase photosynthetic efficiency is still poorly understood.

To investigate the potential of microalgal biomass resource applications, we synthesized CDs with red light emission and fully characterized the resulting physical and chemical properties. C. reinhardtii was used as a model organism. CDs were added to the growth medium as supplements. We found that cell division and biomass of C. reinhardtii increased and major metabolite biosynthesis was accelerated under the 0.5 mg/l-CDs treatment. The regulatory mechanisms acting on C. reinhardtii were investigated systematically using transcriptomics integrated with biochemical analysis. Our analysis showed that the gene regulation of cells induced by CDs involves cell mitosis and the cell cycle, photosynthetic electron transfer and pyruvate synthesis. The above results contribute valuable information related to the production of microalgae bioproducts, which could help alleviate the growing agricultural crisis we face globally.

The synthesis of CDs by the solvothermal method was performed according to Xue et al [29]. Briefly, citric acid (CA, 2.8 g) was dissolved into formamide (FA, 50 mL) and was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave for solvothermal synthesis and then treated in an oven (101-1AB, Faithful, China) at 160 °C for 3 h. After allowing the solution to cool to room temperature, acetone (200 mL) was added and the mixture was placed at −20 °C overnight to allow precipitates to form. Subsequently, the precipitates were rinsed thoroughly with acetone and methanol/acetone solution (1/9, v/v). The mixture was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) to remove the precipitate after cooling to room temperature. The resulting solution was dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500) against ultrapure water and dried at 50 °C to obtain CDs powders. The CDs powders were dispersed in the medium solution by sonication (150 W, 10 min) before experiments.

The morphology of CDs was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, America). The components of CDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (VECTOR-22, Bruker, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK). Fluorescence (PL) spectra and the fluorescence decay kinetics curves were measured by FLS 980 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) and DeltaFlex modular fluorescence lifetime system (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan), respectively. The absorbance of CDs was carried out with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (SP-1920, Shanghai Spectrum Instruments Inc, China). The crystal structure was characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 DISCOVER A25, Bruker, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: C. reinhardtii growth conditions and treatments.
ce:section.ce:para: C. reinhardtii cw15- strain was kindly provided by Fei Wang (Northwest University, Xi’an, China). Algal stock cultures were grown in 130 mL of Tris-acetate-phosphate (TAP) medium on a shaker platform with 120 rpm under ∼ 40 μmol m−2 s−1 light intensity at 25 ± 1 °C until the cell density reached approximately 4 × 106 cells mL−1. For CDs treatment, cells were diluted to 1 × 106 cells mL−1. CDs stock solutions were added to the cell cultures at final concentrations of 0, 0.5, and 5 mg/L. The experimental period spanned 6 days. incubation conditions were kept constant. Three parallel experiments were set up for each treatment.

Both cell density and biomass were measured. Cell density was determined using a hemocytometer (Size:16 × 25, Marienfeld, Germany) under microscopy. Biomass was measured using the weighing method. The specific growth rate was calculated from the dry weight of C. reinhardtii [30] using the following equation:

where DWt1 and DWt2 are the biomass at the incubation times of t1 and t2, respectively. All experiments were conducted in triplicate and averages were reported.

The Cyto-ultrastructure of C. reinhardtii cells after 24 h incubation was observed with TEM (HT7700, Hitachi, Japan). Cells were collected by centrifugation (1500 rpm, 3 min, 4 °C) and fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 4 °C for 4 h. Cells were then washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 M, pH = 7.0) and stained in 1% osmium tetroxide (OsO4) for 2 h at room temperature. Next, they were washed with ddH2O and dehydrated using a gradient of ethanol. Embedding and c-sectioning at 0.07 μm were next performed and cells were double-stained with uranyl acetate and lead citrate. The prepared ultra-thin sections were observed under TEM. Morphological changes in cells were identified by optical microscopy (WYS-08C, Tianjin VIYEE Optical Instrument Co., Ltd, China).

The chlorophyll and carotenoid contents were determined using the colorimetric method [31]. 1 mL of algal cell suspension was centrifuged (6,000 rpm, 5 min, 4 °C). The supernatant was discarded, then the pellets were resuspended in 2 mL of 80% acetone (v/v) and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C. The absorbance of the resultant supernatant was measured at 470, 646, and 663 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The pigment contents were determined using the following equation:

Pigment profiles, including cellular chlorophyll content and cellular carotenoid content, were obtained by calculating the ratio of pigment content to cell density.

The photosynthetic activity of C. reinhardtii was assessed by fast chlorophyll fluorescence kinetics curves using Photon Systems Instruments (AP 110-C, AquaPen-C, Czech Republic). Initially, 2 mL cell samples were incubated in the dark for 20 min to ensure the photosystem II (PS II) reaction center was fully open and the electron transport chain completely oxidized. Then, the samples were measured in an AP110-C detector to obtain chlorophyll fluorescence parameters (see Table 1 for formulas and terms used to define fluorescence parameters).

Protein concentrations were quantified according to the Bradford method using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a standard [33]. Microalgae suspension samples (2 mL) were centrifuged (5,000 rpm, 10 min, 4 °C), and the supernatant was discarded. 1 mL of frozen PBS was immediately added to resuspend the algal precipitate and subsequently crushed by a cell breaker in an ice bath for 20 min. The homogenate was centrifuged (5,000 rpm, 15 min, 4 °C) to get the supernatant for analysis. Quantification of protein content was quantified based on absorbance at 595 nm of a mixture of supernatant and Coomassie brilliant blue dye.

Phenol-Sulfuric Acid was used to detect the carbohydrate content [34]. 5 mL of C. reinhardtii and C. reinhardtii/CDs suspension were centrifuged (8,000 rpm 10 min) and then washed with deionized water. The supernatant was discarded and the pellets were resuspended in 2 mL of distilled water, then extracted in boiling water for 30 min. The extract was centrifuged at 4,000 rpm for 10 min, followed by the addition of 1 mL supernatant with 1 mL 6% phenol and 5 mL H2SO4. The mixture was shaken well, then left at room temperature for 10 min followed by a 40 °C water bath for another 10 min. Quantification of carbohydrate content was achieved by measuring the absorbance at 490 nm of the mixture.

Lipid content was determined by a gravimetric method [35]. 30 mL of C. reinhardtii and C. reinhardtii/CDs cultures were centrifuged at 4,000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitate was freeze-dried before dry weight was recorded. 1 mL of distilled water was added to the algae powder and sonicated for 5 min. Subsequently, 1 mL of methanol was added and shaken at 110 rpm for 1 h. Then, the fat extractant (chloroform: methanol, 2/1, v/v) was added and mixed for 30 min. Following centrifugation at 9,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, the chloroform layer was collected. The above sequence of operations was repeated for the residue. All collected chloroform layers were dried at 40 °C for weighing using a nitrogen blower.

Total RNA was extracted by TRIzol® Reagent (Magen). Paired-end libraries were generated using the ABclonal mRNA-seq Lib Prep Kit (ABclonal, China). The library preparations were sequenced on an Illumina Novaseq 6000 (or MGISEQ-T7), and 150 bp paired-end reads were generated. FastQC was used for the quality check of paired-end reads for each sample. The gene expression level was calculated using the FPKM of each gene based on gene length. Differential expression gene (DEG) analysis was performed using DESeq2. GO function enrichment and KEGG pathway enrichment analysis were also completed using the cluster Profiler R software package. P values less than 0.05 were considered to indicate that the GO or KEGG function was significantly enriched. Transcription factor analysis of DEGs was extracted directly from the PlantTFDB database, or genes were annotated in the Pfam database by the interscan tool.

All data were presented as means ± standard deviation (SD). The data were analyzed using Origin 2019 (OriginLab, Northampton, MA, USA). Statistical analysis was conducted using the SPSS software package (IBM SPSS Statistics 25). Significant differences between the control and treatment groups were determined using ANOVA one-way analysis of variance, and P < 0.05 was considered significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: As exhibited in Fig. 1 a, the CDs displayed monodispersity with an approximate diameter of 3.74 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm (Fig. 1b) corresponded to the (1120) crystal phases of graphene [27]. Fig. 1c showed the fast Fourier transformed (FFT) pattern of CDs. The XRD pattern presented an evident peak at 27° for its (002) planes (Fig. 1d), indicating that CDs existed as graphene-like structures [36].

To understand the chemical composition of the CDs, the FTIR assay was performed (Fig. 1e). The peaks, located at 3377 and 3140 cm−1, demonstrated the presence of hydrophilic hydroxyl and amino groups [27]. The distinct peaks around 2991 and 2798 cm−1 indicated stretching vibrations of CH3 and CH2. The typical peaks at 1650 and 1558 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN, respectively [37,38] The peaks at approximately 1276 and 1205 cm−1 were identified from the CO bonds, whereas the absorption band at 1097 cm−1 was attributed to COC [39,40]. The characteristic absorption bands of aromatic CH appeared at 700–800 cm−1 [41]. These infrared transmittance features confirmed that the CDs had strong water solubility.

XPS analysis indicated that carbon (C 1 s, 283 eV), nitrogen (N 1 s, 398 eV), and oxygen (O 1 s, 530 eV) were present in the CDs (Fig. 1f) [42]. The spectrum of O 1 s band (Fig. S1a) revealed two O forms in CO and CO at 530.7 and 532.1 eV, respectively [43]. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. S1b) revealed three different peaks, at 282.9 (CC), 284.3 (CN) and 286.4 eV (CN) [44]. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S1c), the three peaks at 397.8, 398.6 and 399.6 eV correlated with three different types of nitrogen: pyridinic CNC, pyrrolic C2 NH and graphitic NC3 groups, respectively. Of these, pyridinic nitrogen was the main form of N element present [43,45]. Graphitic nitrogen contributes to quantum yields (QYs) of CDs, which has been described previously [46].

To evaluate the photophysical properties of CDs, UV–Vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) were executed (Fig. 1g). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (black line) showed two distinct peaks. The peak at 350 nm was seen corresponding with n-π* transition related to the lone pair electrons and π* orbitals of nitrogen and CO in the CDs [45,47]. Another absorption peak appeared at 520 nm, which could be ascribed to the typical absorption of an aromatic system [41]. The n-π* transition led to strong FL emission of CDs [48]. CDs presented the maximum excitation spectra (blue line) and emission spectra (red line) at 540 nm and 634 nm, respectively. The FL emission spectra of CDs (Fig. S2) showed a red-shift of the emission peak with increasing excitation wavelength, which indicated that an excitation-dependent property of the fluorescence emission of CDs had supported the luminescence mechanism of the surface states. The FL lifetime decay curve of CDs was given in Fig. 1h. The average lifetime (τ) was fitted as 1.692 ns with tri-exponential decay (Table S1). Reports of CDs have been characterized by good fluorescence stability [18,49]. By dispersing the CDs in deionized water and TAP medium at the ambient environment, we found only 1.22% and 3.03% fluorescence intensity decreased after 7 days of storage, respectively (Fig. S3). Also, the fluorescence was decreased by less than 8.04% after adding the CDs to C. reinhardtii for 6 days (Fig. S4a). These results proved good stability of CDs in our work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs promoted growth in C. reinhardtii by optimizing spectra quality.
ce:section.ce:para: The biomass yield of C. reinhardtii depends on the efficiency of photosynthetic carbon sequestration conversion [50]. Green algae mainly have dual-absorption ranges of red and blue light. red light is the best wavelength for the growth of most microalgae [51]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of C. reinhardtii was presented in Fig. S4b. CDs primarily absorbed green light at ∼ 540 nm, which is poorly utilized by C. reinhardtii. Furthermore, the red fluorescence of CDs overlapped the absorption of C. reinhardtii, facilitating the light utilization of C. reinhardtii. The fluorescence spectra of CDs after co-culture with C. reinhardtii also demonstrated that CDs could act as a constant red light source contributed to the growth of microalgae (Fig. S4a).

To investigate the effect of CDs on the growth rate of C. reinhardtii, we measured dry cell weight (DCW) and cell density. The results showed that the DCW was increased by 6.8% under 0.5 mg/L CDs treatment (Fig. 2 a). However, CDs at 5 mg/L induced a decrease in DCW. The specific growth rate of C. reinhardtii was significantly increased by approximately 52.3% after 0.5 mg/L CDs treatment compared to the control from days 2 to 4 (Fig. 2b). The above results indicated that 0.5 mg/L was the optimum CDs concentration to accelerate the growth of C. reinhardtii. In addition, compared with the control group (Fig. 2c), optical microscopic images of the 0.5 mg/L CDs group showed more algae cells in a single-cell state (Fig. 2d), with cell numbers 10.1% higher by day 3 (Fig. S5), suggesting that CDs accelerated algal cell division.

Recent studies have shown that CDs with red emission are beneficial to plant growth [49,52]. Zhou et al. synthesized a light-harvesting polymer that could accelerate cell division. [53]. In this study, the accelerated growth of C. reinhardtii provided direct evidence that 0.5 mg/L CDs promoted photosynthesis in this species, as demonstrated by an increase in both biomass and cell number. In addition, the optical microscopy results indicated that the cell division process was accelerated. These results suggested that CDs, applied at the appropriate concentration, could help optimize the absorption of red light by algal cells, helping compensate for insufficient light absorption as algal cell density increases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-induced variation of cellular ultrastructure in C. reinhardtii.
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effects of CDs on C. reinhardtii ultrastructure, TEM analysis was performed. Algae cells subjected to the 0.5 mg/L CDs cultivation retained intact morphology, displaying pyrenoids, starches and vacuoles, smooth outer cell surfaces, and clear chloroplast structure, indicating that 0.5 mg/L CDs had a slight influence on the ultrastructure of C. reinhardtii (Fig. 3 a and 3b). However, algae cells in the 5 mg/L CDs group developed uneven cell membranes and distorted cellular interiors. Furthermore, the chloroplast lamellae were blurry and the number of lamellae was decreased (Fig. 3c).

These results illustrated that a high concentration of CDs, rather than enhancing healthy growth, might cause damage to algal cells. The slight growth inhibition effect may be attributed to photodamage caused by the CDs converting excessive available light to the cells, which appeared to disturb photosynthetic function and resulted in excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [54,55]. Meanwhile, the morphology of C. reinhardtii was destroyed due to lipid peroxidation and damage to cell membrane integrity (Fig. 3).

To determine whether CDs promoted C. reinhardtii photosynthetic activity, dynamic characteristics of chlorophyll fluorescence parameters were analyzed. We found that the energy distribution of photosynthesis was altered when CDs were present. Specifically, ABS/RC was decreased (Fig. 4 a), but TRO/RC and ETO/RC were increased (Fig. 4b and 4c). Meanwhile, DIO/RC was reduced (Fig. 4d). Under the CDs 0.5 mg/L treatment, the C. reinhardtii maintained photosynthetic capacity by preferentially ensuring energy capture, transfer and reducing dissipation (Fig. 4e). However, the efficiency of energy absorption, capture, transfer and dissipation increased synergistically in the 5 mg/L CDs group. We presumed that a lower concentration of CDs improved the photosynthetic system light energy utilization efficiency of C. reinhardtii.

Ψo, φEo and φDo denote the ratio quantum efficiency or flux ratios on the receptor side of PS II. Below 0.5 mg/L CDs, the quantum yield distribution parameters (Ψo, φEo) were improved, but quantum yield for dissipation (φDo) was significantly decreased (10.6%) (Fig. S6), indicating that the energy absorbed was mainly devoted to the photochemical reactions, not heat dissipation. Thus, the photochemical reaction efficiency of C. reinhardtii was improved by 0.5 mg/L CDs. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 4f, PIabs was increased by approximately 27.6% in 0.5 mg/L CDs compared with the control, indicating that greater cell viability of C. reinhardtii was maintained at low doses of CDs. In contrast, the slight drop in Ψo and φEo, and the increase in φDo (3.5%) in the 5 mg/L addition revealed a potentially blocked electron transfer in PS II.

Sm, Mo, and N reflect the changes on the receptor side of PS II. The chlorophyll fluorescence parameters of cells on day 2 are shown in Fig. 5 a. For the 0.5 mg/L CDs treatment, VJ was reduced relative to the control, indicating a reduction in the closed active reaction center. Mo was depressed, demonstrating reduced or no QA - accumulation, which facilitated the electron transfer from QA to QB. FV/FO and Fm/FO increased by 2.1% and 2.8%, respectively. However, Sm, N, and VI barely changed. In addition, the above parameters in the CDs-treated (5 mg/L) group showed a similar, though slight, trend to that of the low concentration group, which included reduced Sm, VJ, VI, FV/FO, Fm/FO and increased φDo. Therefore, 0.5 mg/L CDs improved the energy conversion efficiency of C. reinhardtii more effectively. The data on day 4 showed minor differences between the control and low concentration group (Fig. 5b). However, at the 5 mg/L CDs dose, FV/FO and Fm/FO decreased significantly (4.5% and 3.4%), which demonstrated a decrease in the potential activity of PS II and blocked electron transfer. Meanwhile, Mo and VJ increased by 6.3% and 4.9%, respectively, indicating that the accelerated rate of QA reduction led to the accumulation of more QA -. As shown in Fig. 5c, by day 6 the lower dose group (0.5 mg/L) exhibited higher electron transfer relative to the control, mostly reflected in the significant increases of FV/FO and Fm/FO by 16.6% and 12.0%. The result was primarily a consequence of inadequate light absorption due to excessive cell density. More red light was provided by CDs to increase photosynthetic activity and cell vitality. In contrast, the 5 mg/L CDs group had a negative effect on algal cells, consistent with the results outlined above.

Compared to the control, the above results for 0.5 mg/L CDs were supported by the elevated Fv/Fm shown in Fig. 5d. Moreover, the higher level of ΦPSII implied that light energy was more efficiently converted into biologically useful chemical energy via PS II (Fig. 5e). The increase in rETR (Fig. 5f) further revealed that CDs accelerated electron transfer during the photosynthetic process. For the 5 mg/L CDs treatment, the slightly changed Fv/Fm can be attributed to the synergistic increase in energy absorption, trapping, transfer and dissipation in the electron transport chain. According to the above results, 0.5 mg/L CDs delivered red light to C. reinhardtii, which increased the absorption, capture, and transmission of energy by the light system, reduced heat dissipation, accelerated electron transfer, and ultimately improved photosynthetic activity.

Photosynthesis in chloroplasts provides energy and the carbon skeleton for metabolic and cell cycle demands [56]. In the present study, we found that the energy flow of PS II in C. reinhardtii was improved treated with 0.5 mg/L CDs. Cell viability, photochemical efficiency and electron transfer were elevated. Meanwhile, we observed the accelerated electron flow from QA to QB and the increased number of open PS II reaction centers (Fig. 4 and Fig. 5). The increased electron transfer in thylakoid membranes may be attributed to CDs acting as spectral amplifiers. Furthermore, CDs as good electron donors and acceptors, and their excitation by light resulted in the resonance energy transfer of electrons from CDs to chloroplasts [57]. CDs treatment was able to utilize the increased electron transfer, which led to more electrons directed to photochemical reactions instead of dissipated and resulted in a boosted photosynthetic capacity. On the contrary, 5 mg/L CDs changed the energy distribution of the per reaction center of C. reinhardtii (Fig. 4 and Fig. 5), which resulted in the inactivation of the PS II reaction center, damaged the PS II donor side, and reduced the electronic supply.
Based on these results, we speculated that the proper concentration of CDs enhanced photosynthesis by optimizing the absorption spectrum of C. reinhardtii, enhancing the photosynthetic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs promoted the nutritional components of C. reinhardtii
ce:section.ce:para: To further interpret the effects of CDs on cell components and photosynthesis of C. reinhardtii, we measured cell pigment contents and performed cell density normalization. C. reinhardtii exposed to 0.5 mg/L CDs showed an obvious increase in photosynthetic pigments within cells compared to the control (Fig. 6 a-6d). The pigment contents increased most significantly on day 5 (P < 0.05), with 40.4% and 37.9% increases in chlorophyll and total pigment content, respectively. The 5 mg/L CDs exposure showed less of an effect on chlorophyll compared to 0.5 mg/L CDs but with similarly higher carotenoid concentrations. This result may be ascribed to the red light from CDs stimulating the manufacture of photo-protective pigment.

To explore the regulation of the main metabolites of C. reinhardtii after CDs exposure, we measured the contents of protein, carbohydrate, and lipid in algae cells. The metabolites content of the 0.5 mg/L CDs treatment were significantly different from the control. Protein accumulated gradually throughout the first 2 days (Fig. 7 a and 7b), increasing by 22.7%, 28.4%, and 32.7% in protein content, production and productivity, respectively. Carbohydrate production increased from 80.5 mg/L to 95.3 mg/L, and corresponding productivity increased by 21.6% (Fig. 7c and 7d). The 0.5 mg/L CDs group had superior lipid yield, with a rise of 30.5%, 27.7%, and 40.2% in lipid content, production and productivity, respectively, after 1 day (Fig. 7e and 7f). Conversely, the 5 mg/L CDs treatment only induced an increase in protein content and yield in the pre-culture period (day 1), with a weaker effect than the 0.5 mg/L treatment. In addition, the presence of carbohydrate and lipid in cells treated with 5 mg/L CDs was slightly lower than in the control. The above results indicated that a low concentration (0.5 mg/L) of CDs hastens the accumulation of nutritional products, particularly protein and lipid, during the brief incubation period.

Oxygenic photosynthesis, which takes place in chloroplasts, is one of the key processes for carbon fixation. Photosynthetic pigments are involved in light harvesting, triggering the primary photochemical reaction [58]. The emission spectrum of the synthesized CDs corresponded to the absorption wavelength of chlorophyll, increasing the transfer of absorbed photons to the central pigment [59]. Our data showed that with the addition of CDs, the cellular content of pigments and carbohydrate production slightly increased, and the protein and lipid contents significantly increased (Fig. 6 and Fig. 7). These results explained intracellular carbon allocation in C. reinhardtii. We presumed that the minimal increase in carbohydrate content was because the carbon fixed by microalgae was more readily available for intracellular protein and lipid synthesis. Analyses suggested that red light induced lipid production, and resulted in a higher portion of polyunsaturated fatty acids [60,61]. The results explained variations in the form of cellular energy reserves.

The DEGs were subjected to GO and KEGG functional pathway analysis. The results of GO enrichment analysis indicated that DEGs, annotated in three GO categories, were divided into 51 functional groups, including 40 in biological process, 5 in cellular components, and 6 in molecular function (Fig. S8). These DEGs were mainly involved in metabolism, genetic information processing and cellular processes. The enriched GO terms of DEGs in biological processes contained “positive regulation of organelle organization”, “cell cycle phase transition” and “chromosome separation”. The enriched GO terms of DEGs in cellular components contained “nuclear ubiquitin ligase complex” and “anaphase-promoting complex”. Additionally, the results showed that “metal cluster binding” and “iron − sulfur cluster binding” enriched the largest number of DEGs in molecular function.

KEGG functional pathway analysis was performed to comprehensively investigate the universal molecular mechanism of C. reinhardtii exposed to 0.5 mg/L CDs. The pathways with significant enrichment contributed by these DEGs contained the following classifications: (1) Endocrine system (ko04914, progesterone-mediated oocyte maturation; ko04928, parathyroid hormone synthesis, secretion and action; ko04926, relaxin signaling pathway), (2) Cell growth and death (ko04110, cell cycle; ko04111, cell cycle – yeast; ko04113, meiosis – yeast; ko04114, oocyte meiosis), and (3) Metabolism of terpenoids and polyketides (ko00906, carotenoid biosynthesis) and carbohydrate metabolism (ko00030, pentose phosphate pathway) (Fig. 8b). “Biosynthesis of secondary metabolites (ko01110)” and “Photosynthesis (ko00195)” were obtained in the pathways with significant enrichment of up-regulated genes (Fig. 8c). Additionally, in the pathways with significant enrichment of down-regulated genes, “Pentose phosphate pathway (ko00030)”, “Drug metabolism - cytochrome P450 (ko00982)”, “alpha-Linolenic acid metabolism (ko00592)” and “Linoleic acid metabolism (ko00591)” were involved, and the number of DEGs enriched in pentose phosphate pathway was the largest (Fig. 8d). The above results indicated that the identified differential genes were mainly related to cell growth and death, as well as biosynthesis of secondary metabolites.

Based on the results of the physiological analysis of C. reinhardtii and transcriptome sequencing, we mapped the DEGs of relevant genes in the top enriched KEGG terms (Fig. 9 a). A list of all gene names and abbreviations in this work was presented in Table S2. Fig. 9b showed the relationship between gene regulation, differential genes, and key pathways. Genes related to algal cell growth and death, such as anaphase-promoting complex subunit (APC), origin recognition complex subunit 1 (ORC1) and transcription factor E2F3 (E2F3), tended to be up-regulated after CDs treatment. Meanwhile, some genes related to carbohydrate metabolisms, such as 6-phosphofructokinase 1 (pfkA) and transaldolase (talA) of the pentose phosphate pathway, were significantly down-regulated after CDs treatment. In starch and sucrose metabolism, Endoglucanase (EG) and 1,3-beta-glucan synthase (GS) were down-regulated and the expression of granule-bound starch synthase (WAXY) was up-regulated, which was associated with carbohydrate synthesis (Fig. S9). Some genes involved in lipid metabolism, such as phospholipase A2 and 12-oxophytodienoic acid reductase, were down-regulated. Beta-carotene 3-hydroxylase (crtZ), a key enzyme in the carotenoid synthesis pathway, and ferredoxin (Fd) was an important component of the photosynthetic electron transport chain, were both up-regulated in CDs treatment. These results revealed a detailed pattern of gene regulation by CDs on C. reinhardtii.

Microalgae are effective at synthesizing large numbers of metabolites [6]. The amount of secondary metabolites produced is regulated by environmental factors. Increasing evidence suggests that CDs contribute to photosynthetic organisms by regulating growth, promoting nutrient assimilation and boosting resistance to stresses [27,28,62]. However, only a few previous studies have addressed molecular mechanisms underlying the growth stimulation of microalgae [29,63]. In our study, CDs treatment had a positive effect on the growth of C. reinhardtii. During the mitosis anaphase, APC is ubiquitylated and degraded, leading to the release of separase to cleave cohesion, which results in the separation of two sister chromatids [64]. In the present study, CDs treatment significantly stimulated APC expression, suggesting that 0.5 mg/L CDs promoted sister chromatid separation during cell division of C. reinhardtii (Fig. 9). ORC is the DNA replication origin protein of eukaryotic cells. ORC1 is the largest sub-unit of ORC, which is the key factor in regulating cell proliferation [65]. E2F3 controls DNA replication and the expression of genes during cell proliferation [66]. The overexpression of ORC1 and E2F3 indicated that CDs promote the proliferation of algae cells and shorten the cell cycle (Fig. 9). Our results documented a clear increase in the number of algal cells and the difference in division status after the addition of CDs (Fig. 2 and Fig. S5). We confirmed that CDs accelerated the growth and division of algal cells.

Moreover, our results showed that the presence of CDs increased microalgae pigment content and photosynthetic electron transport. CrtZ was significantly up-regulated in the carotenoid synthesis pathway (Fig. 9), suggesting that carotenoid content was altered by CDs, as indicated by the elevation of carotenoid content (Fig. 6). CrtZ is a crucial enzyme for astaxanthin synthesis and is involved in the conversion of β-carotenoids to oxidized carotenoids [67]. In addition, CDs enhanced the photosynthetic efficiency of C. reinhardtii in this study. Fd is considered to be an important component of the photosynthetic electron transport chain, acting as an electron transmitter at the end of the light reaction [68]. The up-regulated Fd correlated with an accelerated conversion of light energy to electrical energy, which further verified the increase in Fv/Fm and rETR (Fig. 5). Therefore, increased photosynthesis efficiency is one of the reasons why CDs enhance the biomass of C. reinhardtii.

Biomass accumulation was promoted when C. reinhardtii was treated with 0.5 mg/L CDs. Endoglucanase (EG), an important component of the cellulase family, hydrolyzes soluble cellulose into oligosaccharides [69]. Down-regulation of EG may lead to cellulose accumulation. 1,3-beta-glucan synthase (GS) is the key enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of UDP-glucose to β-1,3-glucan. Down-regulation of GS gene expression may lead to an increase in UDP-glucose, which is a precursor of starch. Meanwhile, the accelerated conversion of UDP-glucose to starch coincided with upregulating the expression of granule-bound starch synthase (WAXY). Starch is further converted to glucose, which is degraded to pyruvate by glycolytic. Pyruvate, orthophosphate dikinase (PPDK) catalyzes the conversion of phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) to pyruvate (PYR) and releases ATP [70]. PYR is further oxidized to acetyl-CoA to enter the TCA cycle for the metabolism of saccharides, lipids and proteins. The PPDK gene was significantly up-regulated, indicating that the addition of CDs facilitated the synthetic pathways related to PRY, providing more available PRY for carbon metabolism and ultimately facilitating biomass conversion (Fig. 7 and Fig. 9). In addition, TAG lipase (TGL4) can hydrolyze phosphatidylcholine to produce fatty acids [71]. As a substrate for the action of TGL4, phosphatidylcholine is also a major component of cellular and organelle membranes. The presence of CDs significantly down-regulated the expression level of the TGL4 gene, and the corresponding lipid content was increased (Fig. 7e and Fig. 9). Thus, CDs promoted intracellular organic matter synthesis by affecting carbohydrate synthesis and metabolism and providing more pyruvate to the TCA cycle.

We obtained water-soluble CDs with green capture and red emission and applied them to C. reinhardtii. CDs at a concentration of 0.5 mg/L accelerated division and nutrition synthesis of C. reinhardtii cells, ultimately leading to increased biomass. CDs acted as light supplements, accelerating photosynthetic electron transfer and photochemical efficiency. Meanwhile, the expression genes regulated by CDs were associated with processes related to cell cycle, carotenoid biosynthesis, photosynthesis and biosynthesis of secondary metabolites. Specifically, the possible mechanisms by which CDs regulated the biomass of C. reinhardtii are as follows: (1) the expression of genes associated with cell growth and death was up-regulated, which promoted sister chromatid separation of cells, accelerated mitotic progression and shortened the cell cycle, resulting in faster cell growth; (2) the photosynthetic electron transfer-related gene was up-regulated to improve the ability of energy conversion; and (3) the carbohydrate metabolism-related genes were regulated and provided more available pyruvate for the TCA cycle, which led to increased metabolite accumulation and higher biomass. The present work provides the first insight into the molecular mechanisms used by CDs to regulate microalgal biomass, providing theoretical support for expanding the application of nanomaterials in microalgal production, future agricultural and energy. The future study can evaluate in depth the regulatory mechanisms of CDs on specific functional metabolites of microalgae.
